title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
summary:
Mr. Kitcher, an investigator for Scotland Yard, questions Margaret Ernst regarding the history of the Ives couple. Margaret expresses fear and insists that Kitcher promise not to seek out Ambrose Flavard or mention Patrick and Francis Ives to anyone in the village. She explains that a tragedy in 1913 left "invisible wounds" on the residents of Glebeholme, including Nancy Duane and Richard Nigges, and notes that Ambrose is also suffering. Reluctantly, Kitcher swears to keep her words secret, and Margaret begins to recount the history of the Ives family.

Sixteen years prior, a maid working for Reverend Patrick Ives spread a malicious rumor that Patrick was a fraud who used spiritualism to communicate with the deceased in order to swindle villagers out of money. The maid told only one person: Harriet Sipper, a bitter woman who enjoyed exposing the faults of others. Harriet escalated the lie, claiming that Patrick had already defrauded several people.

The rumor specifically targeted Nancy Duane, who had moved to the village in 1910 after marrying the schoolmaster William Duane. In January 1913, Harriet spread the claim that Nancy made secretive late-night visits to the vicar's house while his wife, Francis, was away. Despite the difficulty of verifying such claims in the dark, the villagers of Glebeholme condemned Patrick. This condemnation was supported by Ada Gransbury, a devout woman, and Richard Nigges, a respected lawyer. Nigges claimed that he had personally witnessed a woman resembling Nancy entering and leaving the vicar's house during those times.
